sentences.text: Yeah.
I was born in 1915, in Ostrowiec, Poland.
All my family, my parents, and grandparents, they from-- from the-- the-- the town was like who [NON-ENGLISH].
So everybody was born in this town of Ostrowiec-- in Ostrowiec.
I-- when I was three years old, I was going to the cheder.
And over there was my first husband's sister.
So we were sitting and-- and crying.
So the-- the rabbi, he send my husband, her brother-- my first husband was her brother.
And he told us, don't cry.
The rabbi will [NON-ENGLISH].
So we stopped crying.
And so it was later.
And I was in-- in a higher Hebrew school.
I was going-- and with his sister-- always.
When I was 17 years old, I got married with-- with her brother.
It was-- it was a wonderful marriage.
He-- my-- all my brothers and-- and brother-in-laws all was shocked them-- all of them-- [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], and [NON-ENGLISH].
So-- but my-- my husband then want to-- to be a [NON-ENGLISH].
So he came from the yeshiva back.
And we got married.
He was the first child.
And I was near the-- the last over there.
There was a-- a younger sister now.
So they give-- they-- we got married.
And we bought a farm.
But, of course, his uncles-- everybody got farms.
So we was on a very big farm.
And it was-- we was very rich.
And-- and we got three children-- Yonahla, Sossele, and Nehemiahla.
Until-- till Hitler came in, it was a--
--a wonderful life.
Very religious, yeah-- very religious.
All my family was very religious.
All of them, and my sisters, and [NON-ENGLISH] was-- my mother.
But my-- my father, our father, died young.
He was 51.
And I was then six years old.
So I said Kaddish after my father, because the brothers was in Russia.
And we couldn't come over there to tell them what has happened.
So I-- so brother-in-law took me to the synagogue, and I say Kaddish after my father.
When did you first realize that there is trouble coming?
Yeah.
We-- we realized in-- in the-- in the-- in the-- in '37 and '38.
So we realized that something will happen.
But we didn't know what-- what and from where what-- what till in-- till Hitler come-- came in.
Hitler came in-- in '41.
In '39-- in '39 the Russian took over Ukraine-- in '39.
It-- it was all very-- because the Russian-- we was very rich.
We-- we have to give up everything there.
So it was all very trouble.
But it wasn't so-- so bad.
We-- we gave up a mill with-- with-- with herd, with whatever we had-- cows and horses.
We gave up everything that the Russian took away .
And they want to send us at-- to Sibere.
But I was to give birth my third child.
So they let me-- let us stay till I will give birth to the child.
Until I gave birth-- so it always-- was all very the-- the war came in.
And then we was at the farm.
And then they took us in the ghetto.
And in the ghetto-- and Ostrowiec in the ghetto-- so I was over there.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] In-- in '40-- in '41, Hitler came in.
And in a few months, he-- they-- they took us-- all of us.
They made a Judenrat.
I don't know you heard.
A Judenrat-- the Judenrat came and-- and took us all to the-- took us-- they told us they want to talk something to us about the post office.
But they didn't took us to the post office.
I was with the three children.
And my husband was working.
So they took them from the work.
And they took us to the forest over there.
I was-- the woman with the children he took her-- left.
And the men ride.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were standing this side and a-- and in the left side.
And the men was in the-- in the-- in the right side.
And they start make-- to-- to do the graves.
[NON-ENGLISH]-- the graves.
Then-- but before, they took out all my-- my brother-in-law's-- my father-in-law-- they got the tallisim with that film with them, they took.
So they took the Germans.
And they-- they [NON-ENGLISH] everything and was laughing from-- from our-- of our religion, from everything, they was laughing.
And then he told them to-- to take out the-- the-- to take out the-- the-- the [NON-ENGLISH].
You know what I mean?
Some words is-- all the-- the [NON-ENGLISH]-- naked.
And he start them one after one.
And one he got.
Even a boy from 13 years was belonging to father, and he must be killed.
And-- and this.
And later when they killed all of them.
So they start to-- and to take us back to the ghetto.
The woman with the children and all of us come back to the ghetto.
But none of them come back there.
So my-- my brother-in-law's-- my-- my husband-- every-- everybody was.
I don't cry.
I don't cry.
In the ghetto I was-- I am the only one what I was working in the ghetto-- only one.
And my-- my sisters was already-- one was 35, 40-- in the-- in the-- in the 30s and the 40s, my sister were.
So-- and-- and-- and one sister got nine children.
One sister got seven children; and the older from me, three children, and my three children.
And it-- it was-- it was very bad.
I was working by potatoes.
I took out potatoes.
So I sometime I put in a few potatoes here.
I brought it home.
But it was very bad with food.
So I am the only one what I speak a little English and Ukrainish, of course.
Because they didn't-- they was very good in Hebrew-- all of them only in Hebrew school.
They didn't-- so later, I was only in the Hebrew school.
And I was going out from the ghetto to make a place.
And I was going out from the ghetto, mostly every other night.
Sometime I took-- we took a blouse, sometime a sweater.
And we-- we were up, and I brought a bread.
And-- all of a sudden came the Judenrat-- and told me that I got a farm.
I have to go to work for the Germans, the-- in the Yiddish farms and the Jewish.
So I told them I wouldn't go without my children.
My sisters can-- can go out to bring for their children.
What shall I do?
But I-- so I told the Judenrat I will not go.
And if he will even kill me, I wouldn't leave my three children.
The little one was a year and a 1/2 old then.
So and I didn't come in a few days.
So you have to go out.
I didn't come to-- to go to the-- to-- to-- to work for the fields.
They have to take us and-- with horses, you know, they take us.
So I didn't come.
So my my older boy with-- with my littler girls, they was playing outside.
They saw the-- the German.
German's name was-- I seen the papers-- was [NON-ENGLISH] was [NON-ENGLISH] his name-- [NON-ENGLISH] his name.
He came in.
And my sister was standing.
So he told me [NON-ENGLISH].
So I came in.
I was out.
And I gave the- the little boy-- a-- a year and a 1/2-- still my breast, because I didn't have the food for him enough.
So I gave him the breast.
And I was holding the-- the little boy.
And-- and I see that he holds a cane from-- from [NON-ENGLISH]-- like this.
So I was afraid he will give my sister.
So I came in [NON-ENGLISH].
So he gave me here.
And he gave.
So my child-- also I eat very much a child.
Later I fell down.
And the child and I-- I didn't know about it.
But the Judenrat, they took me, and they took me-- the-- my eyes was watering.
And I was black and blue.
The took me to the farm.
But the-- the Judenrat helped to take out the older two children.
So when I opened in the way a little my eyes-- so I saw I have the two children.
And I know the-- my nanny, her name was Nadia.
She was living not far from us.
So I told her a day before.
She was in the ghetto.
She was bringing me something.
So I told her that maybe I will go in the farm.
So she told me she can hold the-- the two older one.
The boy, he was 8 years old.
And the little girl was 6 and a 1/2 years old.
So-- and so I-- I couldn't-- I wasn't-- I was laying, and they-- they took me out and lay me in the-- in a place, like in a-- I don't know how to say.
[NON-ENGLISH]-- where straw over there.
You know how to-- so you understand?
Yeah.
Good.
But you are a big girl.
And I was-- and-- and she came, Nadia, and she took away the two children.
And I was near three days they didn't took me to work.
Because I was swollen in the eye-- and black and blue.
After three-- after three days, they took me to work.

When I came to the work, and I-- and I was already to the people-- we was over there near 70-- 70 Yiddish-- mostly women-- the two men was though-- and rest, all of us was woman-- what they got also farms.
So I start, what shall I do to take out?
I know the ghetto-- every day, maybe.
But they will kill them all in the ghetto.
I had to take out the-- the-- the-- the little boy.
So I didn't know what to-- what to do.
So when we was in the night, we were sleeping by our little straw over there-- the all 70 over there.
And I asked my neighbor near me in the night, what shall we do?
What-- how can I take out from the ghetto the-- the-- the child?
So she told me, you know, you talk to the-- in [NON-ENGLISH] to-- and the [NON-ENGLISH] took care a Polish-- a-- a Pollack-- yeah.
Yeah.
He was very nice man.
He didn't hit us.
He told us when the Germans has to come-- work, work, work.
Because the German is coming.
Yeah, so-- but he wasn't-- when-- and he-- he knows us.
He was living near our farm not far away-- yeah, very near, yeah.
They was coming to the mill to us.
So they-- it's-- they know us.
So when he was-- so I fell to his feet.
In the-- in the fields.
I fall to his feet.
And-- and-- and tell that I have-- I left my third child over there.
And I have a place for him.
And I-- so he told me that-- he told me that Eva [NON-ENGLISH] survive.
They will know what to do.
He don't want without his wife.
I don't know.
OK.
The second day I-- he came.
And he brought me a paper written in German that the-- the [NON-ENGLISH], the-- the [NON-ENGLISH] want to go to bring the child.
Because she was, for the children, she was all the time with the children.
So-- and she know where in the ghetto they're going-- that her the-- that her sister near the-- lives near the ghetto.
And over there, she has here a little boy-- [NON-ENGLISH].
So-- so she was gone.
And she came.
And my-- my child, two days after I left, the child died.
So the-- the sisters gave me a letter.
This is in-- in the paper, the letter I wrote, and they wrote.
Don't-- don't you cry.
They wrote to me, what you lost, we know this is-- but you know, we also mamas, and we lost also.
But you have two children outside.
Try.
Go in the- the world.
Maybe you will [NON-ENGLISH] might be.
So when she came with other child, [NON-ENGLISH], the two children were so crying.
And they-- the was sitting by the [NON-ENGLISH] under a closet in a-- and through the closet, she gave them eat.
Because she got a 13-year-old girl.
So she did.
And we didn't want her, because her girl can-- can tell somebody but here is Jews.
So they would kill here.
So she give through the closet.
She gave them in, right?
And when I came every night-- when I came, they-- there-- so she took time them out.
The night, here-- here your girl was asleep already in another room.
So she took them out-- the-- the children for me, you know.
No, she told me-- she told me all about, but my sisters wrote to me that please don't cry.
So you were able maybe to-- to-- to [NON-ENGLISH] two children.
It was-- the children was so crying after the little brother.
But it didn't help us too much.
And very soon, the Germans hang up papers of-- of houses, and not to give us Jew a piece of bread-- and not told.
If they will-- some in contact with Jews-- so they will kill them, you know?
And she told me she can't.
She don't want to hold the two children.
And I-- I was thinking-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I-- I was thinking what-- what-- what to do here.
And the other night came two boys-- my sister's-- one 13-years-old, and one-- and one around 16-years-old.
They took-- they-- they took all of them from the ghetto.
One-- one is-- he fall also on the grave with a-- his family.
Every family is together.
So he came out in the night.
He was full of blood.
But he-- he came out.
He was alive, because so many bodies.
So he was alive.
So he came with other children.
Once he came out though the wires in the ghettos-- through the [NON-ENGLISH], you know?
But they came out-- the young-- for 12 years, 13 years, 9 years.
Yeah.
But I got between the children was-- two were-- and she came.
They know at the time and decide maybe-- I will-- can and so on-- out of it.
The-- so when I left them, and I was going to ask Nadia let your-- make for us a grave.
And I will took in these two children.
So when I told her-- so she-- so she told me, how can I do for-- for two-- for children and you?
We-- for five people, I can't do it.
And they will kill me.
They will kill you anyway, she tells me.
So what-- what-- what are the-- they have to kill me too?
From this, I took the children in the forest.
And it start.
It start-- very bad.
In-- in the forest--
In the forest, yeah.
No, no.
In our-- in-- in Galicia, it was-- it was partisans.
In our side wasn't any partisan.
But in a few days came-- came other children from other towns.
So we was aware over there 87.
I-- I was the oldest.
And it came a couple with two children younger than my.
They was by a Pollack, and the Pollack didn't want to hold them.
So they came-- they came in the forest, because-- they was-- I know them from before.
So when the children all come-- so they started to-- to make the graves, the bunkers.
I was for three weeks alone with the two children and three.
Yeah.
And when I was three days, I didn't have any water, any peace of bread.
And I-- I asked Yonah.
He was-- he was then eight years old.
So he was smarter than I am.
I asked him, what shall we do?
Shall I go?
Or you want to go-- what you-- what shall we do?
No, Mamala.
Mamala we called, Mamalatata.
You go, and I will take care of Sossela.
So I did.
This was the third day I went out.
And I, a whole day, went with them.
And I see how to come in the night, go out in the night to come back.
The forest is so big.
What-- what I-- so I try.
I-- I got-- a Ukraine gave me such a shawl-- what they working alone out-- how heavy.
So when I came out, he told me he will sit it at the-- near the-- the tree.
I was alone then with them.
And he was holding Sossela.
I put here a little hat.
And-- and he's-- he want to hold this.
I took her.
And-- and I got a few shmatas-- what Nadia gave me.
So I covered them.
It was snowing a little.
It started snowing in the night.
And I took my shawl and put the place when I go out from the forest.
But when the snow is snow, everything was covered.
When I came around 3:00-- 3:00 in the night or 3:00/3:30 something, and then I didn't know where to come in and where to go out, and where-- where-- where I left my children.
I didn't know.
I couldn't find the-- the same way, because it was snow there.
And I am without stockings and without shoes now.
There was a shmata from the snow.
And so it was still late in the morning.
My-- my face was all torn.
Till today I have with my nose, you see, all torn.
It was the branches.
And I didn't know where to go, where this is such a big place.
So in the morning, a few days before, I was out from the forest-- was over there-- my children was playing in the forest.
And I was sleeping their place a little in the afternoon.
So they heard some-- something-- somebody is, oy, oy, oy.
So-- so-- so they wake me up, and I came.
It was a doctor.
He told me he still was stalking and told me he is a doctor.
Where are my wife?
Where are my children?
And-- and he was bleeding from here and here-- some-- some-- by-- from I don't know how he came in the forest.
And he was laying all with blood.
So I took out my scarf.
Here from one end it was very blooding.
So-- and I-- yeah.
We banded my-- when I was going-- this was already morning.
I recognized the-- the person was.
And I recognize my-- so I came to-- to my children.
And later came the-- the older boys and girls-- all of them 13, 14, 9-years-old, 11-years-old.
And we was 87 over there-- I and a couple what they was with two children smaller there-- younger than my children.
And-- and so we was in the forest.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Oh, this one.
To my children happen-- we was over in the graves-- and with the two children-- the couple.
And I met my children.
We was in there-- all boys and girls.
For the boys, separate graves, and the-- and the girls separate.
In the night, everybody is going looking for bread.
I wasn't-- for-- for two days, I wasn't go-- I-- I didn't go out, because the older lady was with her husband, with the two children.
And she was very, very sick.
And she was bleeding very much.
So at the night, what has happened-- I told, don't you-- I wouldn't let you go out.
My child will bring-- we will together.
And so happened, I was-- I was come very oft to a Czechish couple.
He was working-- the Czech-- was working in our mill.
So I wasn't afraid that they will kill me.
So I used to go to them.
[NON-ENGLISH] I was, I admit-- a few days earlier, I got the last earrings what my mother-in-law gave me when I was engaged.
So she gave me them-- this.
Otherwise, who gave everything up to the Germans-- fur, and jewelry, and everything-- copper-- whatever we got, we have to give them.
So-- and I went.
The night-- she was very sick.
So I sometime, I-- I-- think maybe five wouldn't tell-- maybe she would go out-- get out!
If she would go with me, she would-- she would be alive.
But I-- so she so blooding.
I cannot leave her.
And she was over there when I came.
When I-- when I came to the Czech, she-- oh.
She told me she will bake for.
I gave her the earrings for before-- two to three days.
So she told me she will bake bread, and she would give me.
She always gave me two bottles milk for the children and some fruit.
If she got, she gave it to me.
But I came.
I see them.
And she knows already the time when we-- when I came.
I seem them, the two-- the two was living-- two old people there I know.
And he was working a long time by us on there.
So I see them-- the two of them standing outside.
And I came over-- and what has happened?
And she told me all day with this is a little nearer the-- the forest.
So therefore, they was looking all day for-- for the Jews.
